questions.question: In Flynn's Taxonomy, what distinguishes an SIMD system from an MIMD system? (2 marks)
expectedPoints: SIMD has a Single Instruction stream operating on Multiple Data streams.
MIMD has Multiple Instruction streams operating on Multiple Data streams.
SIMD has centralized, synchronous control, while MIMD has distributed, asynchronous control.

questions.question: Explain the 'lockstep execution' model of SIMD architectures. Discuss one major advantage and one significant challenge associated with this model. (5 marks)
expectedPoints: Definition: Lockstep execution means all Processing Elements (PEs) execute the same instruction simultaneously, synchronized by a central control unit.
Advantage: Simplifies synchronization and reduces control hardware overhead, leading to efficient execution of data-parallel tasks.
Advantage: High throughput for operations on large arrays/vectors (e.g., scientific computing, media processing).
Challenge: Conditional branching (if-else) is inefficient. PEs must often execute all paths, wasting cycles on masked-off operations (branch divergence).
Challenge: Requires problems with regular, data-parallel structure. Irregular problems with data-dependent control flow are poorly suited.
